answer:
**Manusmṛti Chapter 7** presents the most comprehensive treatment of kingship among the three, portraying the king as a divine creation from particles of major gods (Indra, Vāyu, Yama, Sūrya, Agni, Varuṇa, Candra, and Kubera), tasked with protecting all varṇas and āśramas through *daṇḍa* (punishment), which maintains cosmic order.[^1] Key qualifications include humility (learned from aged Vedic Brāhmaṇas), mastery of senses, knowledge of the Vedas, polity (*nītipraśāstra*), logic (*hetuśāstra*), metaphysics (*ātmaśāstra*), and economics (*vārttā*); avoidance of vices like hunting, gambling, women, intoxication, and anger-born faults like violence; and appointment of 7-8 tested ministers, a chief counselor (Purohita), envoys, and supervisors.[^2] The king must deliberate secretly, reside in a secure fort (preferably hill-fort), perform sacrifices, collect tribute justly, and act as a father to subjects while favoring Brāhmaṇas.[^3]

**Viṣṇu Smṛti** offers a more concise parallel, echoing Manu's divine origin and protective role but with less detail on personal qualifications or administration. It emphasizes the king's duty to protect subjects via *daṇḍa*, study sacred texts, appoint learned assessors for justice, and avoid vices; the text aligns structurally with Manu but prioritizes judicial impartiality over elaborate court setup or forts.[^4] No extended treatment of vices or ministers appears in searched chapters (primarily 1-3), suggesting brevity compared to Manu—focusing on *rājadharma* as protection and *dharma*-based rule rather than personal virtues or infrastructure.[^5]

**Bṛhaspati Smṛti Chapter 1** supplements with procedural focus, reiterating the king's divine essence (from the same gods as Manu) for upholding varṇāśrama and resolving disputes via four means (*śruti*, *smṛti*, custom, edict).[^6] It adds the king's active role in courts: entering cheerfully with preceptors/priests, examining cases with 3-7 Vedic Brāhmaṇa assessors (*sabhā* types: stationary/mobile/sealed/directed), a *prāḍvivāka* (interrogator), accountant/scribe; placing symbols (gold/fire/water/*smṛti*) as proof; prioritizing *dharma* treatises over polity; and punishing faulty assessors/witnesses. The king is "head" of the court "body," protector via three means (army/thieves/powerful), taxing justly (1/6th share), and building fortified courts/temples.[^7] Unlike Manu's emphasis on humility/learning/vices, Bṛhaspati stresses judicial machinery (*daṇḍa* as *dharma*), with the king as impartial decider in doubts, superseding groups like guilds.

In synthesis, Manu prioritizes the ideal king's character and setup; Viṣṇu echoes protection/divinity succinctly; Bṛhaspati operationalizes the king as judicial enforcer, adding court hierarchy/proof for *vyavahāra* (procedure)—three complementary voices on *rājadharma* from protection to practice.

[^1]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 7, [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., esp. [p-b46e87](#p-b46e87) to [p-45020b](#p-45020b), [p-afbfa2](#p-afbfa2)–[p-81b1fc](#p-81b1fc), [p-0952cb](#p-0952cb)–[p-cf8d7c](#p-cf8d7c).

[^3]: Ibid., [p-2f8219](#p-2f8219)–[p-ae198e](#p-ae198e), [p-69b6a8](#p-69b6a8)–[p-99f678](#p-99f678).

[^4]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, Ch. 3 (relevant per corpus pointers), aligns on *daṇḍa*/duties; cf. [Ch. 3 translation/](works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/03/translation/).

[^5]: No exhaustive qualifications/vices/ministers in Ch. 1-3; corpus searches yielded sparse hits.

[^6]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Ch. 1, [p-cb5a36](#p-cb5a36)–[p-4128d6](#p-4128d6), [p-bf73f8](#p-bf73f8), [translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/).

[^7]: Ibid., [p-7409b9](#p-7409b9)–[p-e91140](#p-e91140), [p-05bf04](#p-05bf04)–[p-d8a012](#p-d8a012), [p-96e3a0](#p-96e3a0)–[p-500986](#p-500986), [p-998d72](#p-998d72)–[p-9429ac](#p-9429ac).
